data.title: Barclays to sell stake in British payments business to Brookfield
data.description:
LONDON (April 17): Barclays has agreed terms for Brookfield Asset Management to buy most of its British payments business, the bank said on Thursday, ending a more than year-long effort to offload the business which processes payments for retailers.

Under the complex terms of the deal, the two parties will create a standalone entity for the business, into which Barclays plans to invest approximately £400 million (US$528.2 million or RM2.3 billion), Barclays said.

The British bank remains the sole shareholder in that unit for the first three years, but Brookfield may acquire an approximately 70% ownership interest, the bank said, on top of its initial 10% stake, and subject to certain conditions.

Those include Barclays recovering the full value of its investment into the partnership. The bank said it expects to retain the remaining 20% stake.

Barclays has been trying to offload all or part of the business since December 2023, having said it was no longer the best owner for the unit which processes payments on behalf of retailers.

The British bank follows other lenders in cutting back investments in the payments processing business, which is becoming a scale game dominated by fewer players.

Valuations in European payments firms have fallen amid concerns over revenue outlooks at companies such as Worldline, Nexi and Adyen.

Barclays initially sought a valuation of more than £2 billion for the whole business, Reuters has reported, but found few takers given the technological complexities of any such deal.

The business provides critical infrastructure to the UK economy, processing billions of pounds of payments annually for small businesses as well as domestic and international corporate clients, Barclays said.

The deal will not have any material impact on the bank's financial guidance or targets, it said.

Uploaded by Magessan Varatharaja

data.title: Stocks edge higher in cautious trade, US-Japan talks in focus
data.description:
(April 17): Emerging Asian equities ticked higher on Thursday as investors assessed trade negotiations between the US and Japan although concerns over the economic impact of US trade policies kept sentiment in check.

The MSCI index of emerging Asian equities climbed as much as 0.8% following an over 1% drop on Wednesday, while a gauge of Asean stocks advanced for a seventh straight session to a two-week high.

South Korea's Kospi index rose as much as 1% as the central bank held its interest rate steady, as widely expected, and signalled a rate cut in May.

Taiwan stocks shed 0.7%, dragged by top contract chipmaker TSMC, which fell 1% despite forecasting robust growth in 2025 and reporting an estimate-beating quarterly profit.

Stocks in Asia consolidated at the start of the week but tumbled on Wednesday after the US unveiled curbs on some Nvidia chip exports to China, amplifying trade tensions between the world's two biggest economies.

Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell's warning on the risks of a growth downturn and a rise in inflation sent Wall Street indexes spiralling overnight.

Traders are keenly watching the outcome of US-Japan trade talks, though the direction of discussions with China remains the biggest overhang.

Kenneth Tang, senior portfolio manager at Nikko Asset Management, said a long-drawn-out negotiation period would be negative for a lot of export-oriented countries in Asean, with Singapore, Thailand and Malaysia being the most affected.

"Uncertainty is always the worst recipe for markets, and I think that's going to drive a more challenged environment for markets in the second half of this year," Tang said.

In Southeast Asia, Singapore stocks jumped 1.6% to a nine-session high. The city-state's banks were the top gainers in the benchmark, alongside Singapore Telecommunications (SingTel) and ST Engineering which gained around 2%.

"This suggests the flight to quality rotation and that the market is taking a view they are safer and more resilient than the rest of the market."

SingTel and ST Engineering have risen 10% and 5%, respectively, so far this month, with the latter trading near an all-time high.

Benchmark gauges in Indonesia and Malaysia gained 0.5%, while Thailand's key equities gauge was slightly down.

A recent rally in emerging Asian currencies fizzled out as the dollar held steady against a basket of major peers.

Analysts widely predict the recent gains in most Asian currencies will be short-lived as tariff-induced growth headwinds and the knock-on effect of a weak Chinese yuan intensify depreciation pressure.

Singapore's dollar slipped from a near six-month high hit earlier in the session. Thailand's baht was anchored around its late-October highs as gold's sizzling rally supported the unit.

Uploaded by Magessan Varatharaja

data.title: Pernod Ricard's 3Q sales miss forecasts
data.description:
LONDON (April 17): Pernod Ricard on Thursday reported a 3% decline in third-quarter sales, missing forecasts as tariff uncertainty rocks the already-struggling spirits sector.

Analysts had expected Pernod, the world's number two Western spirits maker, which produces Jameson Irish whiskey and Mumm champagne, to report a 2% decline in organic net sales in the three months to end March.

Pernod said its results were affected by customs clearance procedures and a production interruption in India, the late timing of Easter, tariffs affecting cognac sales in China, and a sharp decline in its travel retail division due to the suspension of duty-free sales of cognac there.

CEO Alexandre Ricard told Reuters that had it not been for a technical interruption at a bottling plant in India and the late timing of Easter — neither of which will remain a factor in the next quarter — Pernod's results would have been in line with expectations.

On tariffs, Pernod had observed no change in underlying demand in the US, but it was hard to predict if that would change, Ricard continued.

Pernod left its full-year guidance for a low single-digit decline in sales unchanged, but warned the environment "remains challenging and very fluid with regard to tariffs".

It had previously cut its 2025 outlook as well as its growth forecast for 2027-2029, citing geopolitical uncertainties such as tariffs.

US efforts to rewrite the global trade relationships have plunged the entire spirits sector into uncertainty. Pernod and other cognac makers separately face tariffs in China.

It put the annual impact of tariffs at €200 million (US$227.2 million or RM1 billion) in February.

Escalating trade tensions also threaten consumer sentiment at a time when the appetite for pricey liquors is already under pressure.

Spirits sales have been falling industry-wide as growth in the US in particular retreats from the post Covid-19 boom, compounded by high interest rates and inflation.

Pernod's US sales rose 2% in the quarter, helped by increased orders ahead of expected tariffs.

That was offset by declines in Europe, China and its travel retail division, where sales tumbled 31%.

Uploaded by Magessan Varatharaja

data.title: Chinese stocks face risk of US$800b US outflows, Goldman says
data.description:
(April 17): US investors could be forced to offload around US$800 billion (RM3.53 trillion) of Chinese equities “in an extreme scenario” of financial decoupling between the world’s two largest economies, Goldman Sachs Group Inc estimates.  

Currently around 7% of market capitalisation of Chinese companies’ American Depositary Receipts, or ADRs, are held by US institutions that may not be able to trade in Hong Kong, Goldman analysts including Kinger Lau wrote in a note on Wednesday. That means such investors may not be able to go to the Asian financial hub to purchase shares if companies like Alibaba Group Holding Ltd face an involuntary delisting from the US.

Goldman is joining a group of global banks that have started assessing the worst outcome for investors as the once-unthinkable prospect of a financial divorce between the US and China grows with an escalating trade war. Concerns about American stock exchanges kicking Chinese firms out, which became an issue during US President Donald Trump’s first term, have resurfaced after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent’s recent comment that all options are “on the table” in trade talks with China.

“The extreme levels of uncertainty in the global trading system have led to extraordinary volatility in the global capital markets, and concerns about global recession and decoupling risks between the two largest economies globally in other strategic cohorts,” the analysts wrote. They estimate that in a forced delisting scenario, ADRs and the MSCI China Index could see 9% and 4% valuation drawdowns from current prices respectively. 

US institutional investors currently own about US$250 billion worth of Chinese ADRs, or 26% of the total market value, according to Goldman. Their exposure to Hong Kong stocks amounts to US$522 billion or 16% of the market’s total. They own about 0.5% of China’s onshore equities known as A shares. Together, that amounts to more than US$800 billion worth of Chinese shares.

It may take just one day for US investors to complete sales of their A shares, while it may require 119 and 97 days to exit Hong Kong stocks and ADRs respectively, Goldman estimates.

Meanwhile, in the same extreme scenario, Chinese investors might need to unload their US financial assets, which could amount to US$1.7 trillion, they wrote, adding that around US$370 billion would be in equities and US$1.3 trillion in bonds.

Among US passive funds, the Kraneshares CSI China Internet Fund, the largest China-focused exchange-traded fund in the US, could be more impacted in the event of forced liquidation due to ADR delisting, Goldman said. The fund has the highest weighting of ADRs at 33%, half of which do not carry Hong Kong listings, as well as a 72% ownership by US investors, the bank’s analysts added.

Earlier, JPMorgan Chase & Co estimated that ADR delistings could lead to removal from global indices, resulting in aggregate passive outflows of around US$11 billion. 

Uploaded by Tham Yek Lee

data.title: Nvidia CEO stresses importance of China market in Beijing visit, Chinese state media reports
data.description:
BEIJING (April 17): Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said on Thursday that China was a very important market for Nvidia after the US imposed a ban on sales of its H20 artificial intelligence chips to the country.

"We hope to continue to cooperate with China," Huang said in a meeting with Ren Hongbin, head of the China Council for the Promotion of International Trade, according to China state broadcaster CCTV.

Huang arrived in Beijing earlier in the day at the invitation of the trade organisation.

His visit comes at a time when the United States has put new restrictions on China-bound shipments of Nvidia's H20 data centre GPUs, the only compliant AI chip the company can sell legally to China.

The US ban has created uncertainty for Chinese internet companies, which were still anticipating H20 deliveries by the end of the year.

Nvidia didn't immediately respond to a Reuters' request for comment on Huang's agenda in China.

Uploaded by Magessan Varatharaja

data.title: TikTok adds 'community notes' feature to combat misinformation
data.description:
(April 17): TikTok is testing a new “Community Notes”-style feature, called Footnotes, to add more context to posts that may be misleading or inaccurate.

Footnotes will appear alongside videos on the app to “add helpful details that may be missing”, according to a company blog post. Those additional details are crowd-sourced from the app’s user base. Footnotes will only appear if they have been rated “helpful” by the company’s algorithm, which will take into account whether “people who usually have different opinions” agree on the note’s context, the post read. 

The new labels will not replace TikTok’s fact-checking programme, which limits the distribution of content deemed inaccurate by third-party fact-checkers. 

TikTok is the latest social media service to employ a feature like Footnotes. X has had a version of the product, called Community Notes, since before Musk acquired the company in late 2022. Meta Platforms Inc, which owns Instagram and Facebook, has also adopted this approach and eliminated its fact-checking programme earlier this year. 

TikTok will test Footnotes in the US to start. The app, which is owned by Chinese parent ByteDance Ltd, remains in flux as it faces a potential US ban. ByteDance must divest from the app’s American operation by mid-June in order to satisfy a national security law.

Read also:
Meta saw TikTok as ‘highly urgent’ threat, Zuckerberg says at antitrust trial

Uploaded by Tham Yek Lee

data.title: ECRL on track for completion by December 2026, says MRL CEO
data.description:
PASIR PUTEH (April 17): The East Coast Rail Link (ECRL) project is now on track for completion by December 2026, with the entire alignment from Kota Bharu to Selangor recording work progress of 81.07% as of last month.

Work progress of the project stood at 90% in Terengganu, and 86% in Pahang.

Malaysia Rail Link Sdn Bhd (MRL) chief executive officer Datuk Seri Darwis Abdul Razak said the Kelantan government had played an important role in ensuring the smooth implementation of the project, especially in resolving issues related to land acquisition.

"Work progress of the 43km alignment in Kelantan has reached 88.86%, contributing significantly to the progress of the overall project. The testing and commissioning process is expected to begin in June 2026.

"Track laying works have also shown positive developments, with significant progress achieved in Kelantan, Terengganu and parts of Pahang involving the 400km alignment from Tunjong, Kota Bharu, to Maran in Pahang," he said.

He told reporters this after inspecting the progress of the project at the Pasir Puteh ECRL Station site here on Thursday.

Darwis said the construction of two ECRL stations in Kelantan, namely in Tunjong and Pasir Puteh, also recorded encouraging progress, reaching 69% and 68% respectively.

Work on both stations is expected to be completed by May next year.

According to him, most of the issues raised by residents, including flash floods in Pasir Puteh, have been addressed through engagement sessions with the Land and District Office, local authorities and the state government since last year.

“We are committed to resolving every issue to ensure that this project benefits the people and can be completed on schedule,” he said.

The 665km ECRL project crosses the East Coast states of Kelantan, Terengganu and Pahang before connecting Selangor on the West Coast of Peninsular Malaysia.

The ECRL from Kota Bharu to the Gombak Integrated Terminal is expected to be completed on schedule in December 2026, and begin operation in January 2027.

Uploaded by Tham Yek Lee

data.title: Malaysia Aviation Group warns of higher fares as costs surge from US tariff
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Malaysia Aviation Group Bhd (MAG), the parent of national carrier Malaysia Airlines, warned that the global supply chain pressures from the new US tariff could raise its operating costs by up to 25%.

MAG is monitoring costs, particularly in aircraft components, as some are subject to tariffs when shipped from other countries to the US, group managing director Datuk Captain Izham Ismail said in a press conference during the group’s results presentation.

“Naturally, there will be cost pressure, the cost of sales will go up, and naturally, businesses will pass on the cost to customers,” Izham said. “I [am being as] candid as possible.”

The US has paused the implementation of a sweeping set of tariffs for 90 days while imposing a 10% tariff on all countries and individualised reciprocal higher tariffs on nations with which the US has the largest trade deficits.

China, Malaysia’s biggest trading partner, alone faces up to a 245% tariff on imports to the US.

Malaysia Aviation Group Bhd group managing director Datuk Captain Izham Ismail: With costs of sales going up, fares have to go up. Our concern is the willingness to pay by customers to travel — it becomes an issue. (Photo by Mohd Izwan Mohd Nazam/The Edge)

While MAG will continue to advocate for efficient cost management at its companies, there are fears of lingering supply chain issues, Izham flagged.

Global carriers, including Malaysian airlines, are grappling with supply chain disruption that has delayed deliveries of new aircraft. Maintenance activities have also been affected by shortages of qualified parts and rising demand for repairs.

“With costs of sales going up, fares have to go up. Our concern is the willingness to pay by customers to travel — it becomes an issue,” he said.

Still, MAG does not intend to seek fresh capital from its largest shareholder Kazanah Nasional Bhd, Izham said. “We don't want capital injection. Our guiding principle is to operate MAG to be commercially sustainable,” he noted.

To date, MAG had only drawn down RM1.3 billion from a total of RM2.3 billion pledged by Khazanah in 2020, leaving large reserves to manage economic volatility if needed, Izham said, adding that the group could still count on Khazanah for support during severe macroeconomic disruptions.

MAG's cash balance stood at RM3 billion as at end-December 2024.

Read also:
Malaysia Aviation Group posts RM54m earnings for FY2024, its second straight annual profit

Edited ByJason Ng

data.title: Sunsuria gets development rights for RM492 mil project in Kwasa Damansara
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Sunsuria Bhd (KL:SUNSURIA) signed an agreement with a unit of the Employees Provident Fund for the development rights to affordable housing in Kwasa Damansara.

Under the agreement with Kwasa Land Sdn Bhd, Sunsuria will pay about RM88.63 million for the 9.46-acre land in the township, Sunsuria said in an exchange filing. The upcoming development will feature 520 residential units with an estimated gross development value (GDV) of RM492 million, it said.

“It is with great enthusiasm and optimism that we welcome Sunsuria to our list of distinguished partners who have chosen to work with us in building Kwasa Damansara,” Kwasa Land managing director Datuk Adenan Md Yusof said in a joint statement.

The project will be delivered in phases, with the first phase slated for launch in the third quarter of 2026. The entire Kwasa Damansara covers more than 2,200 acres of mixed development that represents Klang Valley’s last significant greenfield project.

“Our development in Kwasa Damansara will offer a thoughtful mix of terraced housing and high-rise residences, designed to support multi-generational living,” said Sunsuria’s chief executive Tan Wee Bee.

To date, Kwasa Land has granted development rights to 16 partners for 780 acres within the Kwasa Damansara, or about 59% of the township's total saleable land area of 1,331 acres with an estimated GDV of nearly RM40 billion.

Sunsuria shares paused for the midday trading break at 39 sen on Thursday, valuing the group at RM349 million ahead of the announcement.

Edited ByJason Ng

data.title: Global pharma, AI firms potential early movers into Johor-Singapore SEZ, says IRDA director
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Companies from China, Denmark, and Australia are among the early potential investors showing interest to set up shop in the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ).

Some prospective investors have made multiple visits and are exploring setting up integrated operations through the recently established Iskandar Malaysia Facilitation Centre, according to the Iskandar Regional Development Authority (IRDA).

“The ones that come frequently with their delegates, and especially when the parent companies are involved, indicate serious intent,” said Maimunah Jaffar, the IRDA's director of strategic driver. “These players typically bring an entire ecosystem with them.”

Set up in February, the Iskandar Malaysia Facilitation Centre is a one-stop centre that facilitates and coordinates the end-to-end investment-related process, which includes applications and approvals, investment facilitation, and aftercare and reinvestment within the JS-SEZ.

The entity was also designed to facilitate the JS-SEZ aspirations to achieve 50 major investment projects for the first five years of its establishment. So far this year, the centre has already received over 250 enquiries from potential investors.

Maimunah was speaking during a panel discussion at the 32nd National Real Estate Convention on Thursday.

Some of the investments could be announced as soon as this year, she said, noting that the Danish firms are particularly strong in the medical and pharmaceutical sectors, while Chinese investors are exploring opportunities in artificial intelligence (AI).

The potential investment scope is significant, with some foreign companies eyeing up to 1,000 acres of space within Iskandar Malaysia, and their move could bring in up to 50 supporting firms along with them, forming a cluster-based investment model, Maimunah said.

“We are seeing interest from embassies and business representatives of companies based in Singapore that are looking to relocate or expand into Johor,” she said, adding that “we are looking to provide tailored incentives” for sectors such as pharmaceuticals and medical devices.

Edited ByJason Ng

data.title: Petronas Lubricants-Quaker Houghton partnership to boost industrial metalworking sectors in Malaysia, India
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Petronas Lubricants International (PLI) and industrial process fluids company, Quaker Houghton, have formed a strategic partnership to provide products, solutions and services for key industrial sectors in Malaysia and India.

A joint statement on Thursday said both companies could leverage their product portfolio and geographic market position and provide an expanded range of industrial solutions for customers.

“Beginning in the second quarter of 2025 (2Q2025), PLI will be the exclusive distributor of Quaker Houghton’s portfolio of world-class metalworking fluids in the transportation and industrial end markets in Malaysia.

“In India, Quaker Houghton will offer PLI’s range of high-performance maintenance lubricants and industrial fluids to its steel mill customers,” it said.

PLI group chief strategy and transformation officer Domenico Ciaglia said PLI is committed to building long-term alliances to provide greater product availability and service quality, strengthening its position as the partner of choice in global industrial lubricants.

“The team at Quaker Houghton are uniquely positioned to unlock opportunities with us, both in terms of reaching new markets and in being the partner we need to lead the industry into a new era of effective, sustainable service,” Ciaglia said.

Meanwhile, Quaker Houghton executive vice president, global specialty and chief growth officer Jeewat Bijlani said the partnership enables the company to provide its metalworking fluids to a greater number of customers, noting that Asia is one of its key markets.

“PLI has the proven experience, reputation, and channels to support the customer requirements and expand our reach in Malaysia,” said Jeewat.

According to PLI, the partnership reinforces the companies’ shared vision of driving industrial solutions excellence while meeting the evolving needs of modern manufacturing, as both companies will partner and set new standards in performance for industrial applications.

Uploaded by Lam Seng Fatt

data.title: Malaysia, China to deepen strategic cooperation in four key sectors — joint statement
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Malaysia and China will deepen economic cooperation by focusing on four key sectors — the digital, green, blue, and tourism economies — with efforts aimed at enhancing integration across industrial, supply, value, data, and talent chains.

In a joint statement on building a high-level strategic Malaysia-China community with a shared future, both countries agreed to become a pacesetter for regional cooperation on new quality productive forces, promote the Belt and Road Initiative Cooperation Plan’s implementations (signed in 2024), and further align development strategies.

The joint statement was issued following Chinese President Xi Jinping’s three-day state visit to Malaysia, which concluded on Thursday, at the invitation of the King, Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar.

It said both sides spoke highly of the nearly thousand-fold increase in bilateral trade volume in the past 50 years since the establishment of diplomatic relations and committed to jointly implementing the Five-Year Programme for Economic and Trade Cooperation (2024–2028) to consolidate the outcomes of economic and trade cooperation and expand the trade scale.

Both parties agreed to collaboratively establish a safe and stable industrial and supply chain, enhancing cooperation. Malaysia-China will promote mutual investment and strengthen exchanges in high-value, high-growth industries.

This strategic focus will attract quality investments, accelerate technology adoption, and strengthen Malaysia’s role in global supply chains, according to the statement.

It also highlighted that the two sides will promote the upgrading of the “Two Countries, Twin Park” cooperation, support the high-quality development of economic and trade cooperation zones, and provide a better business environment for their enterprises.

Recognising the importance of a high-level bilateral investment agreement in promoting investment cooperation, the two sides acknowledged the possibility of future engagement to explore ways to enhance the liberalisation and facilitation of two-way investment.

They also plan to improve the level of investment protection within the context of the current bilateral investment agreement.

Both parties will jointly explore cooperation on new quality productive forces, create new growth points in cutting-edge areas such as advanced manufacturing, artificial intelligence, and quantum technology, deepen smart city cooperation, and strengthen the integrated development of industrial and supply chains.

In addition, Malaysia welcomes Chinese enterprises to participate in the country’s 5G network construction.

Both sides look forward to exploring potential cooperation in the semiconductor industrial chain to the extent practicable, to maintain the stability of the industrial and supply chains.

Malaysia and China acknowledged that e-commerce is a vital catalyst for trade and economic growth, innovation, and the development of micro, small, and medium enterprises.

Building upon the long-standing collaboration in this area, both sides will deepen cooperation in the e-commerce ecosystem to contribute towards greater, sustainable economic linkages and bring about tangible benefits to both economies.

The parties will continue to encourage enterprises to promote cooperation in the field of new energy based on business principles, and jointly create new forms of the green economy.

They will explore and promote cooperation on rail equipment, aerospace and nuclear power, support Malaysian airlines in introducing and operating Chinese commercial aircraft, and facilitate the industrial upgrading and energy transformation of both countries.

The two nations also agreed to enhance cooperation on infrastructure connectivity, jointly implement key projects such as the East Coast Rail Link, promote rail-sea transportation, and improve regional connectivity.

Both countries encourage their ports to strengthen cooperation and facilitate blue economy development.

Both sides are committed to exploring and creating more strategic and iconic cooperation projects based on mutual trust, mutual benefit, and win-win results.

They are committed to comprehensive aviation cooperation, and will strengthen the technical exchange of civil aviation expertise and enhance aviation logistics cooperation between the Kuala Lumpur International Airport and Zhengzhou Xinzheng International Airport to realise the common vision of a seamless “Air Silk Road” between Asean and China.

Malaysia and China reaffirmed their commitment to strengthening rail transportation and infrastructure cooperation and contributing to the realisation of the Pan-Asian Railway vision.

Uploaded by Tham Yek Lee

data.title: Chinese tea firm Chagee raises US$411 mil in New York IPO, Reuters reports
data.description:
SYDNEY (April 17): Chinese tea firm Chagee has raised US$411 million (RM1.82 billion) by setting the price of its shares at US$28 each in its New York initial public offering, according to two sources with direct knowledge of the matter.

The sources could not be identified discussing confidential information. Chagee did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Chagee launched the deal last week as financial markets grappled with the prospect of a global trade war resulting from US President Donald Trump's tariffs package.

The deal comes as Chinese companies listed on US stock exchanges could face the prospect of being delisted as part of the US response to tensions with China. US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant last week did not rule out removing Chinese stocks from US exchanges, adding that all options were on the table.

The final price for the 14.7 million American depository shares sold was at the top of the US$26 to US$28 per share range flagged to investors when the deal was launched. At that price, Chagee will be valued at US$5.1 billion, according to its regulatory filings.

Cornerstone investors subscribed for Chagee's shares worth up to US$205 million sold in the IPO, filings showed.

Chagee warned of some hit from the tariffs in its prospectus, but said "cross-border trade" was not its principal business. All of its products are manufactured in China.

The tea brand generated 29.5 billion yuan (US$4 billion or RM17.83 billion) in sales last year, with over 6,000 teahouses worldwide at the end of 2024. Nearly all of its stores in China are under a franchise scheme.

Chagee has expanded across Southeast Asia, entering Malaysia, Singapore and Thailand, according to its prospectus.

Uploaded by Chng Shear Lane

data.title: TSMC's quarterly profit surges but Trump policies cloud outlook
data.description:
TAIPEI (April 17): Taiwan's TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker, on Thursday logged a forecast-beating 60% leap in quarterly profit, though its outlook has been complicated by US President Donald Trump's unpredictable trade policies.

Benefiting from surging demand for advanced chips used in artificial intelligence applications, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co said net profit for January-March climbed to T$361.6 billion (US$11.1 billion or RM49.11 billion), its fourth straight quarter of double-digit growth.

That was ahead of a T$354.6 billion LSEG SmartEstimate drawn from 18 analysts.

Trump's trade policies and threats to put tariffs on semiconductors have created much uncertainty for the global chip industry and TSMC, whose customers include Apple and Nvidia.

TSMC announced plans for a US$100 billion US investment with Trump at the White House last month, on top of US$65 billion pledged for three plants in the state of Arizona, one of which is up and running.

TSMC's shares have dropped 20% so far this year given uncertainty about US trade and tariff policies.

Uploaded by Chng Shear Lane

data.title: T7 Global gets five-year contract from Petronas
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Oil-and-gas services firm T7 Global Bhd (KL:T7GLOBAL) said on Thursday it has secured a five-year contract for an undisclosed value from Petronas Carigali Sdn Bhd.

The contract designates T7 Global’s wholly owned unit Tanjung Offshore Services Sdn Bhd as a panel contractor for engineering, procurement, construction, and commissioning services for remote operations, the company said in a statement. The appointment is effective from Feb 28 until 2030.

The scope of work includes design engineering, facility modification, asset integration and system commissioning, T7 Global said. The company said it would still have to participate in mini-bidding during the appointment to get work orders.

As a panel contractor, T7 Global said it will play a key role in supporting Petronas’ efforts to transform and digitalise its assets, an initiative aimed at enhancing efficiency and safety in offshore operations.

“This appointment reflects Petronas’ commitment to digital transformation,” said T7 Global executive chairman Tan Sri Nik Norzrul Thani N Hassan Thani. “We look forward to contributing to Petronas’s digital transformation journey over the next five years.”

In recent months, T7 Global has secured multiple key awards, including maintenance, construction and modification as well as jack-up rig contracts while its industrial solution division secured a job from Malaysia’s Defence Ministry to provide radar systems.

“These contracts will provide a steady cash flow for the group, forming a strong financial foundation to support our ongoing expansion and long-term growth plans,” Nik Norzrul added.

T7 Global posted a 21.4% jump in net profit to RM40.33 million in 2024 on the back of an 11.2% increase in revenue to RM647.15 million, thanks to a boost from its diversification into the industrial solutions business.

Edited ByJason Ng

data.title: Cover Story: LTAT: A retirement fund like no other in Malaysia
data.description:
This article first appeared in The Edge Malaysia Weekly on April 7, 2025 - April 13, 2025

WHAT comes to mind when you hear “Lembaga Tabung Angkatan Tentera”?

Some may view the Armed Forces Fund Board (LTAT) as a retirement fund akin to the Employees Provident Fund (EPF) but one that’s bespoke for the Malaysian armed forces. While not entirely incorrect, this school of thought is not wholly accurate either.

Recruits who begin their career with the Malaysian Armed Forces (MAF) — be it the Malaysian Army, Royal Malaysian Navy or Royal Malaysian Air Force — pay a monthly defined contribution to LTAT under their initial 12-year employment. This comprises 10% of their monthly salary, while another contribution equal to 15% of their monthly salary would be paid into the fund by the government as the employer — much like the EPF, albeit with differing contribution rates.

Beyond the initial 12-year term, “other ranks” — enlisted personnel and non-commissioned officers (enlisted personnel who have risen through the ranks rather than being commissioned), who comprise the bulk of the armed forces and are referred to as servicemen under the Tabung Angkatan Tentera Act 1973 — can qualify for subsequent three-year extensions.

This is where LTAT’s retirement system differs from that of the EPF. Once servicemen have a 21-year verified service record, they are eligible for pension. Regular commissioned officers are pensionable with 20 years of verified service or when they reach the voluntary or compulsory retirement age. Short-term commissioned officers are pensionable at the compulsory retirement age.

The MAF’s compulsory retirement age ranges from 50 to 60 based on rank, while the voluntary retirement age ranges from 40 to 50 based on rank and gender. Other ranks or servicemen retire from the armed forces at their end-of-service date.

Once a soldier is pensionable, he still contributes to the LTAT until he is discharged from service. However, the government will no longer contribute its share of monthly contributions, and prior government contributions to the soldier’s LTAT account will be returned to government coffers.

Full withdrawal from LTAT is eligible a month after discharge from service, reaching 50 years of age or upon the contributor’s death. A retired soldier’s monthly pension, which is paid by the government, is calculated based on a fraction (differs for other ranks, regular commissioned officers and short-term commissioned officers) of their total verified months in service multiplied by the final salary.

Contributions from other ranks to LTAT have always been compulsory, while contributions from commissioned officers only became mandatory for those commissioned after Jan 1, 2016 (compulsory officers).

Of LTAT’s 117,574 total members at end-2024, 110,142 or 94% constituted other ranks or servicemen, 4,936 (4%) were compulsory officers and 2,496 (2%) were officers making voluntary contributions. The monthly contributions of voluntary contributors are capped at RM2,000.

Only 6.5% or 7,669 members are non-active members; that is, those who have retired and no longer contribute but opted to not fully withdraw their savings in LTAT.

This “hybrid” retirement system is what makes LTAT unique, says its chief executive Mohammad Ashraf Md Radzi, while explaining the government’s move back in 1973 to have a specific retirement fund for servicemen under the MAF.

“It starts off as a defined contribution and ends up with a defined benefit. With all this, liability projection is not easy.

“With the EPF, it’s age-based. Anytime you have a baseline income, you apply the ruler for age, and you already know when your liability is going to come,” says Ashraf. “With us [LTAT], we have in our portfolio a basket of contributors who are on contracts for 12, 15, 18, 21 or 26 years. On top of that, a set for officers only became compulsory in 2016. So, liability projection is anyone’s guess.”

 

Save by subscribing to us for your print and/or digital copy.

P/S: The Edge is also available on Apple's App Store and Android's Google Play.

data.title: CTOS Digital may log softer 1Q earnings but rebound in sight in 2Q — BIMB Securities
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): BIMB Securities Research anticipates a potential dip in credit reporting agency CTOS Digital Bhd's (KL:CTOS) first-quarter results, but projects a subsequent rebound in business momentum starting in the second quarter.

In a note on Thursday, the research house said that an earnings miss — if any — for the first quarter ended March 31, 2025 (1QFY2025) should be seen as a tactical buying window.  

BIMB maintained its positive outlook for CTOS for FY2025, viewing it as a period of growth driven by key catalysts expected to emerge from 2QFY2025.

The research house added that structural trends — such as the expansion of digital financial services, increasing credit participation, and sustained institutional demand for risk analytics — remain unchanged. 

Economic conditions across CTOS' key operating markets remain robust, with forecasts for gross domestic product (GDP) growth and business activity indicating continued expansion.   

The structural demand for credit — and by extension, credit data — continues to strengthen, supported by advances in digital financial inclusion and sustained domestic consumption, which remain a central driver of GDP in these economies, according to the note.

BIMB said that US is not a significant trading partner for the markets in which CTOS operates, making concerns related to potential US political developments — such as the so-called "Trump risk" — largely immaterial to the company’s outlook. 

The firm added that CTOS offers a more attractive dividend yield, and a lower price-earnings ratio compared to its industry peers. At a share price of RM1.12, the firm sees a 19% upside potential.

The stock is trading at a 23% discount to global peers on a price-to-free cash flow (P/FCF) basis, despite the company’s stronger structural growth drivers and superior projected return on equity, said the house.

BIMB cautioned that investors focusing solely on potential 1QFY2025 earnings risks may overlook the broader long-term growth story.  

“Investors overly focused on 1QFY2025 risk missing the forest for the trees,” the firm said. 

BIMB further reiterated its 'buy' recommendation for CTOS, maintaining its target price of RM1.33, based on 30 times FY2025 forecast P/FCF — broadly in line with peer benchmarks.        

In FY2024, CTOS' net profit fell by 10% to RM106.27 million, down from RM118.11 million a year ago, despite a 16.6% increase in cumulative revenue to RM304.85 million from RM261.44 million. 

Meanwhile, CTOS' net profit fell 42.2% to RM32.39 million for 4QFY2024, from RM56.01 million a year ago, largely due to higher tax expenses arising from the absence of a tax credit. Revenue for the quarter increased 5% to RM76.82 million from RM73.15 million in 4QFY2023. 

CTOS declared a fourth interim dividend of 0.99 sen per share — payable on April 30 — bringing its total dividends for FY2024 to 3.25 sen per share, versus 3.33 for FY2023. 

According to Bloomberg, 11 analysts, including BIMB, recommend 'buy', indicating a bullish sentiment. 

At the time of writing on Thursday, CTOS shares were down one sen or 0.9% at RM1.11, valuing the group at RM2.56 billion. Year to date, the stock has retreated 7.5%. 

Read also:
Absence of tax credit drags CTOS Digital’s net profit 42.2% lower in 4Q

Edited ByIsabelle Francis

data.title: Cover Story: LTAT moves forward despite challenges
data.description:
This article first appeared in The Edge Malaysia Weekly on April 7, 2025 - April 13, 2025

SHORTLY before Hari Raya Aidilfitri, Lembaga Tabung Angkatan Tentera (LTAT) announced a dividend of 5.25% for 2024 — the highest it has paid out in seven years. The total distribution of RM514.2 million made up 87.5% of the armed forces fund’s net profit for the year.

While the dividend payout is a great improvement from the 2% in 2018 and 2.5% in 2019, there is still much to be done for LTAT to return to its former glory.

The key question is whether the armed forces fund is using internally generated funds or taking on borrowings to make the dividend payout.

LTAT chief executive Mohammad Ashraf Md Radzi, who took office a year ago in April 2024, tells The Edge in an exclusive interview that the armed forces fund finances its dividend payments entirely through internally generated funds, without any reliance on borrowings or external working capital financing.

“The declared dividend is credited to members’ accounts and will only be disbursed upon withdrawal at the end of their service. LTAT does not distribute dividends in cash, thereby mitigating exposure to significant cash withdrawals related to dividend payouts. Additionally, LTAT maintains adequate liquidity at all times to meet withdrawal obligations through available cash reserves and liquid assets,” says Ashraf, who joined the armed forces fund as chief financial officer.

“Our dividend payments are prudently managed through internal financial resources, ensuring sustainable and responsible financial governance,” he adds, noting that, unlike Permodalan Nasional Bhd (PNB) which has a proprietary fund to tap into for dividends should the need arise, LTAT does not have such a fund.

Ashraf says LTAT’s asset rebalancing and diversification exercise, anchored on its Strategic Asset Allocation Framework (SSA), remains a priority as it ensures the sustainability of the fund while staying aligned with market conditions and global fund management standards. The framework was drawn up in 2020 and introduced in 2021.

LTAT’s assets under management had grown to RM13.6 billion at end-2024, from RM9.26 billion in 2020 (see table), while its reserves exceeded the RM1 billion mark, from RM45 million in 2018. Despite this, sceptics remain cynical about the armed forces fund, largely because of its chequered past.

When asked how the past year at the helm has been, Ashraf says, “The appointment [as chief executive] was effective April 1, but it doesn’t feel like it has been a year. It feels like it has been much longer.

“It [LTAT] needs a lot of work. Looking at how things were looked after previously, it certainly isn’t a two- or three-person job. It needs a management team to look after the whole thing.”

Later he adds, “Hopefully with the dividend that we announced, I will still have about a billion [ringgit] in reserves — so we will be able to handle any shocks that may come.”

Ashraf’s trepidation is understandable, considering the concerns at LTAT and the missteps over the past two years which saw his predecessor, Datuk Ahmad Nazim Abd Rahman, leave in January 2024.

While Ashraf has brought some semblance of stability to the armed forces fund, he has been at the helm for only a year. And LTAT has had four chief executives in the last seven years.

Ashraf also realises the difficulty in growing its portfolio size given the minimal increase in members each year due to the fixed size of the Malaysian armed forces — an issue that his peers at other government-linked investment companies do not encounter. 

“The way the government runs the armed forces — at about 120,000 in strength — is flat, maintained. Each year, about 7,000-odd leave, who are replaced by about 7,000. So, it’s net flat. When it comes to managing the inflows and outflows, we are also about net flat. All the withdrawals are supported by the inflows, new contributions,” he explains as he talks about the challenges he faces in running the retirement fund.

“Usually, it’s a small gap, which is very manageable by us. But that creates a different challenge for us at LTAT. Our capital is flat. If you compare with the Employees Provident Fund, Kumpulan Wang Persaraan (Diperbadankan) (KWAP) and even Lembaga Tabung Haji, all of them are operating on a net positive cash inflow each month, or even if not each month, each year for sure — whereas our capital is flat. So, I need to sweat my assets all the time.”

In 2024, LTAT saw 7,879 new members, while 7,574 members fully withdrew their retirement savings. Contributions stood at RM1.01 billion versus RM1.17 billion in withdrawals — coming out to a net withdrawal of RM161 million.

Challenges at Boustead Holdings

At the top of LTAT’s list of assets is its flagship Boustead Holdings Bhd, which it took private in June 2023 for RM703.25 million, or 85.5 sen per share for the remaining 40.58% equity interest in the company.

Barely four months later, in October 2023, the armed forces fund forked out RM1.11 billion, or RM1.55 per share, to buy out another listed entity — Boustead Plantations Bhd (BPlant) — via Boustead Holdings. This deal came about after the original plan to partner with Kuala Lumpur Kepong Bhd (KL:KLK) to jointly own the company hit a snag.

This U-turn resulted in Ahmad Nazim leaving LTAT, attributed as well to a planned restructuring being put on hold following a change at the helm at the Ministry of Defence, from Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan to current minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin. LTAT chairman General Tan Sri Raja Mohamed Affandi Raja Mohamed Noor also resigned, possibly echoing the dissatisfaction over Ahmad Nazim’s departure.

The restructuring was critical, considering that the assets of the armed forces fund and Boustead Holdings were facing financial difficulties at that point. LTAT has 54.9% stake, via Boustead Holdings, in Pharmaniaga Bhd (KL:PHARMA), a Practice Note 17 (PN17) company; a 72.38% stake in ailing Boustead Heavy Industries Corp Bhd (KL:BHIC); and a 22% stake in Affin Bank Bhd (KL:AFFIN).

LTAT and Boustead Holdings together sold nearly 27% of their stake in Affin Bank, the seventh largest banking group in the country by assets, to a Sarawak state entity last year to reduce its debts.

Also in the armed forces fund’s portfolio are Boustead Petroleum Marketing Sdn Bhd, better known as BHPetrol; the Royale Chulan hotel chain; and some parcels of land, among other assets.

BHIC, which mustered a meagre RM7.57 million in net profit on RM237.14 million in revenue for its financial year ended Dec 31, 2024 (FY2024), has been plagued by the ghost of the controversial RM9.13 billion littoral combat ship project that had to be taken over by the Ministry of Finance. As at end-2024, the company had accumulated losses of RM463.77 million.

Meanwhile, Pharmaniaga has been grappling with a restructuring exercise since falling into the PN17 category, which was brought about by a RM552.3 million impairment due to unsold Covid-19 vaccine. The large impairment dragged the group into its deepest quarterly net loss of RM664.39 million, on RM862.72 million in revenue, in the fourth quarter ended Dec 31, 2022 (see story on Page 61).

Ashraf explains his predicament. “I think throughout the Boustead [Holdings] group, one of the key challenges that we discovered when we began the transformation was the mismatch of debt, mismatch of debt profile — you have your various instruments, term loan, RC (revolving credit), sukuk … but your selection of these debt instruments is on the basis of what you use it for.

“So, within the group, what we discovered was that there were a lot of mismatches, short-term revolving credits that were utilised for purchases of assets that carried long gestation periods. So, these are the things that did not help the business, because suddenly you’re firefighting in your business operations and your profitability is going towards debt servicing.”

Boustead Holdings’ debt levels

While Boustead Holdings is asset-rich, it has been crumbling under the weight of its debts.

In November last year, Public Accounts Committee chairperson Datuk Mas Ermieyati Samsudin highlighted concerns about LTAT’s financial instability and the suspension of a restructuring plan dubbed Project Moonshot. The PAC expressed its fears that prolonged inaction could force the armed forces fund to liquidate its assets, which could result in a cash flow crunch, multiple financial challenges and erode up to 50% of its funds, thus putting nearly RM5 billion of its contributors’ money at risk.

A check with the Companies Commission of Malaysia reveals that in FY2023, Boustead Holdings had total liabilities of RM7.43 billion, of which RM6.73 billion were current liabilities due in a year. Among the reasons to take the company private is to expedite its debt restructuring exercise and get it back into shape, as being a listed company with the many processes and shareholder approvals required could slow things down.

Boustead Holdings’ sale of a 27% stake in Affin Bank to the Sarawak government brought in about RM1.78 billion, which was largely used to pare down its debts.

On Boustead Holdings’ debt levels, Ashraf says: “The debt is down to about RM1.6 billion. It was about RM3.4 billion before. So, of the RM1.6 billion, RM600 million-plus are the perpetuals (perpetual bonds), which is very expensive. That is the next, technically, we target to resolve. We can’t afford to continue having something that pays out 13% [in interest].”

Boustead Holdings’ perpetual sukuk of RM1.2 billion was approved by the Securities Commission Malaysia in mid-November 2013. The perpetual sukuk is accounted as equity because there is no contractual obligation to redeem the instrument.

From Boustead Holdings’ last annual report, which is for FY2022, the perpetual sukuk amounted to RM607 million and had rates of between 9.75% and 11.6%. It is also stated that if the company did not exercise its option to redeem the instrument at the end of fifth year, the periodic distribution rate would increase by 1.5% per annum in the sixth year. And from the seventh year onwards, the periodic distribution rate would be increased by another 1% per annum for every year thereafter, subject to a maximum of 15% per annum.

News reports have it that pilgrims’ fund Lembaga Tabung Haji is the largest holder of the perpetual sukuk, with RM175 million in the Islamic debt paper, followed by LTAT with RM150 million and KWAP with RM100 million.

Nevertheless, while LTAT is not cash-strapped, some of Boustead Holdings’ assets may be hived off. The proceeds will be used largely for reinvestment purposes and recapitalisation.

“What is strategic to us … there’s pharmaceuticals, there’s plantations … To an extent, what we have done well historically is defence, even though it’s very transactional or contractual in nature. Properties, we’re in there for the long run by virtue of our large holdings in Mutiara Damansara,” says Ashraf.

From Moonshot to Earth

At end-February, defence minister Mohamed Khaled mentioned that LTAT and Boustead Holdings would undergo a restructuring under a new plan dubbed Project Earth. In a written parliamentary reply, he said: “The objective [of Project Earth] is to enhance operational efficiency and productivity while ensuring LTAT’s subsidiaries remain competitive in their respective industries.”

In truth, this was the first time many had even heard of Project Moonshot, and it came as a surprise that it was being replaced so soon.

Ashraf says, “Over the past six months through all the PAC (Public Accounts Committee) sessions, there was a lot of news coming out from it, questions asked on Moonshot, under my predecessor [Ahmad] Nazim. What I’ve gone on to do is transition that into Project Earth, where I think I’ve grounded it in terms of the intent of what our real strategic businesses should be. And we believe that under our stable, our gem is Pharmaniaga (see accompanying story) despite its financial challenges, and the other one is [Boustead] Plantations.”

While details on Project Earth are scarce, what is known is that it is a refinement taking into consideration the need to nurture key companies with long-term benefits and growth prospects.

On how Earth came about from Moonshot, Ashraf says, “I think it’s not the case of what went wrong, but about preference — how Moonshot has transitioned into Earth. The main difference is the question of keeping Boustead Holdings. Whereas Moonshot was with the idea of shutting down Boustead Holdings. I think it’s easier said than done. When the company was carrying RM3.4 billion of debt, and even up to this point when we’ve addressed the MTNs (medium-term notes) that were due, there’s still RM1.6 billion [in debt] left. Even if we embark on maybe another round of monetisation that raises RM600 million to RM800 million, or even a billion, no lender would take too kindly to the asset-stripping of the entity where it has exposure.”

Project Earth was presented to the cabinet on Dec 13, 2024, and its guiding principles include improving operational efficiency and productivity, ensuring that subsidiaries remain competitive in their respective industries, with each operating company structured under the most optimal shareholding.

In a nutshell, Project Earth is aimed at reducing LTAT’s strategic assets to 35% and reinvesting in more liquid assets, with a target to increase the fixed income allocation to 20%, by 2028.

Boustead Plantations’ transformation

The plan to hive off BPlant and retain a small stake has changed after the company was taken private. There is a government guarantee from the finance ministry for the RM2 billion loan LTAT took to buy the 32% stake not held by Boustead Holdings.

“I think the guidance from the government is, ‘Look, you’ve held on to land-based assets for a long time. So, instead of taking the easy route of just selling it off, why don’t you take care of it?’ I think that’s the message that I understand. So I’ve then cleaned up … We’ve put in a new management team who is very hands-on, who goes down to ground … Plantations are not rocket science. They need attention. We have so many good plantation companies, both GLCs and private ones. So naturally there is talent, there is technology available for us to tap into. So what it needs is attention, the right level of attention from management all the way up to the number one,” says Ashraf.

BPlant owns and manages 42 oil palm estates and 10 palm oil mills across Malaysia. It has a total land bank of 97,500ha, of which 74% is planted.

In early November last year, Eco World Development Group Bhd (KL:ECOWLD), via its 81%-owned subsidiary Mutiara Balau Sdn Bhd, sought to buy 10 parcels of land measuring 847.25 acres in Semenyih, Selangor, for RM742.41 million from BPlant. It is looking at a new mixed-use development called Eco Forest 2, which will have an estimated gross development value of RM4.6 billion.

“We are assisted by having a few pockets of estates that are already mature for development, that are no longer suitable for plantations. Recently, what we did with EcoWorld gives us the monetisation that we need to reinvest in catching up on replanting and all the fertilising and cleaning up of the estates,” says Ashraf.

“What we need to do is concentrate on nurturing it [BPlant] over the next two to three years. I think we will find ourselves in a good position to relist it.”

The recent success of Johor Plantations Group Bhd (KL:JPG) could have been the catalyst for this plan. Johor Plantations’ initial public offering in March 2022 raised RM1 billion, with the public portion oversubscribed by 2.18 times.

Whether Ashraf will be at the helm of LTAT to witness the changes coming to fruition remains to be seen. But the changes under Project Earth, if implemented, could be a game changer for the armed forces fund. 

 

Save by subscribing to us for your print and/or digital copy.

P/S: The Edge is also available on Apple's App Store and Android's Google Play.

data.title: Malaysia delays LNG shipments due to outage at export plant, Bloomberg reports
data.description:
(April 17): Malaysia is delaying liquefied natural gas (LNG) shipments to buyers due to an outage at its massive export facility, threatening to curb supply in Asia.

Petronas, Malaysia’s state-owned exporter, has requested delays for more than five scheduled shipments affected by the outage, said the traders, asking not to be identified as they were not authorised to speak with media.

At least three production trains were offline at Petronas’ Bintulu export facility, according to Kayrros, which gathers operational information via satellites. The outage was detected on April 9, it said.

Petroliam Nasional Bhd, as the state-owned company is formally known, didn’t respond to a request for comment.

This is the latest in a string of outages across Asia, which could support spot prices of the power plant and industrial fuel. Brunei LNG and the Ichthys facility in Australia were forced to reduce output earlier this month for unplanned maintenance.

Uploaded by Tham Yek Lee

data.title: Ex-Goldman banker Roger Ng could testify in Najib’s 1MDB-Tanore criminal trial
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Former Goldman Sachs banker Roger Ng Chong Hwa might testify in former prime minister Datuk Seri Najib Razak’s 1Malaysia Development Bhd-Tanore (1MDB-Tanore) criminal trial.

Deputy public prosecutor Kamal Bahrin Omar told trial judge Datuk Collin Lawrence Sequerah that the prosecution had received Najib’s application on Monday, April 14.

“The prosecution can make arrangements to facilitate Ng to come to the court as a witness for a specific date. We could make the arrangement, subject to the prosecution giving the particulars of the arrangement,” he said on Thursday.

Najib’s lead counsel Tan Sri Muhammad Shafee Abdullah informed the court that they had submitted the subpoena to call Ng as a witness, and it was addressed to the director general of the Witness Protection Division of the Prime Minister’s Department, Khairul Anwar Zakaria @ Abdul Hamid.

“The defence wants to call Ng, and has no interest in calling the director general. However, as the prosecution is willing to cooperate, we need time to interview Ng first, before deciding on calling him as a (defence) witness,” Shafee said.

Following this, Kamal Bahrin said the prosecution is withdrawing its application to object to the calling of the Witness Protection Division director general.

Sequerah then struck out the prosecution’s objection.

Ng, a Malaysian, was sentenced by a court in the US in 2023 with 10 years’ imprisonment after being found guilty of conspiring to violate US anti-bribery laws and conspiring to launder money with regard to 1MDB.

He was sentenced on March 11, 2023, by US district judge Margo Brodie, after jurors found him guilty of helping his former Goldman Sachs boss, Tim Leissner, embezzle money from 1MDB.

However, Ng has been back in Malaysia since October 2023, through an arrangement with the US Department of Justice (DOJ).

Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail has said that the DOJ agreed to repatriate Ng for authorities in Malaysia to continue investigations into 1MDB or Goldman Sachs.

High Court registrar called over Ng’s DNAA

Following the development, High Court deputy registrar Nurul Ain Hamzah, the defence’s 21st witness, was called by Shafee to testify over Ng’s charges from the Sessions Court to the High Court.

Nurul Ain said that on Dec 17, 2018, they received approval from then-attorney general Tan Sri Tommy Thomas to charge Ng for Securities Commission Malaysia offences.

The case, she added, was then transferred to the High Court, and then Ng was granted a discharge not amounting to an acquittal (DNAA) on Jan 9, 2023, by judge Datuk Muhammad Jamil Hussin.

Following that, Shafee asked for the proceeding notes that led to Jamil granting the DNAA. However, Nurul Ain said the notes could only be issued by the High Court.

After a short break, Nurul Ain said the defence should make a formal application before Jamil to request for the court recording on Jan 9, 2023.

“Following that, they can request the recording and transcript of the proceedings,” she said.

When asked by Shafee, the witness said this could be done by next week, and the court could also endorse and verify the court documents related to the matter.

Najib is on trial on four counts of abuse of power and 21 counts of money laundering of a sum of RM2.27 billion of 1MDB funds.

The former prime minister is currently serving his jail sentence after being found guilty of charges in relation to SRC International Sdn Bhd, a former subsidiary of 1MDB.

The 1MDB-Tanore trial resumes next Monday.

Read also:
Ex-Goldman banker Roger Ng gets 10 years' jail over 1MDB scandal
Former Goldman banker Roger Ng expected to return on Saturday night to help in 1MDB probe — Saifuddin Nasution
Roger Ng given DNAA while Malaysian court awaits conclusion of his American trial

Edited ByAniza Damis
